title: Reading Data from CSV and Text Files
description: Learn to import data from CSV and other text files using base R and readr.

slides.title: Why Import Data? 📂
slides.content:
# Getting Data into R

Most real-world analysis starts with data stored in files. R can read data from many formats. The most common is **CSV** (Comma-Separated Values).

We'll cover:
- Base R functions: `read.csv()`, `read.table()`
- Tidyverse functions: `readr::read_csv()` (faster, better defaults)

slides.title: Reading CSV with Base R
slides.content:
Base R provides `read.csv()` which is a wrapper around `read.table()` with sensible defaults.

```r
# Read a CSV file
df <- read.csv("path/to/file.csv")

# Common options:
# header = TRUE  (first row as column names)
# sep = ","      (separator)
# stringsAsFactors = FALSE  (don't convert strings to factors)
# na.strings = c("NA", "") (values to treat as NA)
```

If your file uses a different separator (e.g., tab), use `read.table()` or `read.delim()`.

slides.title: Base R Example
slides.content: We'll create a small CSV on the fly to demonstrate. In practice, you'd read from a file.
slides.codeSnippet:
# Simulate reading a CSV (using a text connection)
csv_text <- "name,age,score
Alice,25,88
Bob,30,92
Charlie,22,85"
df <- read.csv(text = csv_text, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
print(df)
str(df)

quizData.question: What does `stringsAsFactors = FALSE` do in `read.csv()`?

options.text: Prevents character columns from being converted to factors

quizData.explanation: It keeps character columns as character, not factors.

slides.title: Reading with readr (tidyverse)
slides.content:
The `readr` package (part of tidyverse) provides fast and consistent functions:
- `read_csv()` for CSV
- `read_tsv()` for tab-separated
- `read_delim()` for custom delimiters

Benefits: faster, never converts strings to factors, prints progress, and returns a tibble.

```r
library(readr)
df <- read_csv("file.csv")
```

slides.title: readr Example
slides.codeSnippet:
library(readr)
csv_text <- "name,age,score
Alice,25,88
Bob,30,92
Charlie,22,85"
df <- read_csv(csv_text)
print(df)
str(df)

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: Use `here::here("data", "file.csv")` for reproducible file paths. The `here` package finds your project root.
